coredata.dc:title: 3D-carbon dots decorated black TiO2 nanotube Array@Ti foam with enhanced photothermal and photocatalytic activities
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Chaorui
Li, Dong
Li, Yangsen
Li, Ning
Zhang, Fen
Wang, Yanzhong
Chang, Qing
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Nanostructured titanium oxide semidconductors are considered as potential materials for photothermal and photocatalytic applications. However, investigation on usage of titanium oxide nanotube array lay behind other titanium oxide nanomaterials. In order to change this uneven situation, we reported herein that carbon dots decorated black TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam (CDs/Black TNA@Ti) can work as highly efficient device for solar-driven water evaporation and pollutant degradation. Initially, TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam (TNA@Ti) was prepared through anodization of Ti foam. Through subsequent hydrogenation treatment and carbon dots decoration, CDs/Black TNA@Ti was finally achieved. It was found that CDs/Black TNA@Ti demonstrated three characters including excellent solar light absorption ability, wettability and hierarchical porous structure. For these advantages, photothermal property of TNA@Ti was stepwise improved. In solar-driven water evaporation experiment, water evaporation rate and solar thermal conversion efficiency attained 1.762 kg m−2 h−1 and 55.3% respectively for CDs/Black TNA@Ti, which are higher than previous reports on titanium oxide nanotube array. CDs/Black TNA@Ti also exhibited higher photocatalytic property than other samples without hydrogenation and/or carbon dots decoration. The CDs/Black TNA@Ti is promising for practical applications in solar-driven water evaporation and purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanotube
Carbon dots
Photothermal
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of TNA@Ti and Black TNA@Ti
Solar-driven water evaporation and pollutant degradation tests

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the fabrication process of TNA@Ti, Black TNA@Ti and CDs/Black TNA@Ti; (b) Schematic illustration of the solar-driven water evaporation experiment by the as-prepared samples; (c–f) Low- and high-magnification SEM images of the as-anodized TiO2 nanotube array on Ti foam.

(a, b) Digital photographs of TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti; (c) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti; (d–e) Low- and high-magnification TEM images of TNA@Ti; (f–g) Low- and high-magnification TEM images of Black TNA-300@Ti.
(a) XRD patterns of TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti; (b) EPR spectra of Black TNA-300@Ti; (c–d) Ti2p and O1s XPS spectra of TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti.
(a–b) TEM images of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti; (c–d) STEM image and corresponding energy dispersive spectroscopy map of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti.

(a) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti; (b–c) The shape of water droplets on the black TiO2 nanotube array and carbon dots decorated black TiO2 nanotube array.

(a) Surface temperature increment of various samples under 2.0 kW m−2 simulated solar irradiation over time; (b–e) Surface IR thermal photographs of bulk water, TNA@Ti, Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti under 2.0 kW m−2 simulated solar light irradiation for 30 min; (f) Evaporated water mass changes for various samples under 2.0 kW m−2 simulated solar irradiation over time; (g) Water evaporation rate and solar thermal conversion efficiency of various samples under 2.0 kW m−2 simulated solar light irradiation.

(a) Photocatalytic performance of photofading, TNA@Ti, Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti for degradation of rhodamine B; (b) Photocatalytic performance of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti with various additives in rhodamine B solution under simulated solar light illumination; (c) Fluorescence spectral changes of terephthalic acid solution observed during illumination of TNA@Ti, Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti.

(a) Cycle performance of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti under solar illumination at 2 kW m−2; (b) Survey XPS spectra of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti with and without 20 times cycling experiment; (c) TEM image of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti after 20 times cycling experiment.

head.ce:title: 3D-carbon dots decorated black TiO2 nanotube Array@Ti foam with enhanced photothermal and photocatalytic activities

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266590, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266590, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanostructured titanium oxide semidconductors are considered as potential materials for photothermal and photocatalytic applications. However, investigation on usage of titanium oxide nanotube array lay behind other titanium oxide nanomaterials. In order to change this uneven situation, we reported herein that carbon dots decorated black TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam (CDs/Black TNA@Ti) can work as highly efficient device for solar-driven water evaporation and pollutant degradation. Initially, TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam (TNA@Ti) was prepared through anodization of Ti foam. Through subsequent hydrogenation treatment and carbon dots decoration, CDs/Black TNA@Ti was finally achieved. It was found that CDs/Black TNA@Ti demonstrated three characters including excellent solar light absorption ability, wettability and hierarchical porous structure. For these advantages, photothermal property of TNA@Ti was stepwise improved. In solar-driven water evaporation experiment, water evaporation rate and solar thermal conversion efficiency attained 1.762 kg m−2 h−1 and 55.3% respectively for CDs/Black TNA@Ti, which are higher than previous reports on titanium oxide nanotube array. CDs/Black TNA@Ti also exhibited higher photocatalytic property than other samples without hydrogenation and/or carbon dots decoration. The CDs/Black TNA@Ti is promising for practical applications in solar-driven water evaporation and purification.

ce:section.ce:para: As the source of our human life, water is indispensable resource for natural world. More than 70% of the earth's surface is covered by water, and 2.53% of the water is fresh water drainable for our human. Unfortunately, due to the technical restriction, only 0.34% of global fresh water storage is closely related to our human life [1]. Moreover, with the accelerated industrialization of human society, the problem of water pollution is becoming increasingly serious. So water scarcity is a global issue that needs to be seriously considered. Until now, many methods of water treatments, which includes water disinfection, decontamination and desalination, offer hopes in mitigating the impact of impaired waters around the world [2]. For example, desalination technologies, which include membrane separation, thermal desalination et al., can be used reliably to increase fresh water supply from seawater and saline aquifers [3,4]. However, a great deal of current treatment methods are often chemically, energetically and operationally intensive. In seawater desalination area, many technologies typically requires the conveyance of seawater to desalination plant, pretreatment of intake water, disposal of the concentrate (brine), and process maintenance, high cost and environmental concern are involved. So these methods cannot be used in many developing countries for the lack of appropriate infrastructure. Therefore, new-generation technologies are being pursued to purify water without intensive use of chemicals, fuels and capital.

With regarding to the energy-intensive problem, two futuristic water purification methods, which include photocatalysis and solar driven water evaporation, came out with the primary use of solar energy−the most abundant renewable energy source. The annual solar energy striking the surface of the Earth is an order of magnitude greater than all the estimated non-renewable energy resources, including nuclear and fossil fuels [5]. Due to the continuous research on solar energy technologies, the usage of solar energy has made remarkable progress. Photocatalytic reactions on TiO2 has attracted tremendous interest over the last 30 years. The material of TiO2 has an almost unique set of properties, and can absorb the ultroviolet light to produce highly reactive chemical species (e.g., hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals). The reactive species can be used to degrade the pollutant inside water [6,7]. Further, in 2011, Chen et al., prepared black hydrogenated TiO2 nanocrystals, and attained substantial photocatalytic activities under solar light irradiation [8]. On the heels of photocatalysis, harvesting solar energy for water evaporation (solar driven water evaporation) has also been developed to achieve water purification. Solar irradiation of one sun can provide much larger energy than the required energy for evaporation of 1 kg water per square meter per hour [9]. It has also been reported that more efficient and cost-effective solar-driven water evporation can be achieved by localizing photothermal materials on air-water interface (“air-water interface solar heating concept”). Under solar light irradiation, the photothermal materials can confine heat at the evaporation surface, heat loss from hot zone to bulk water can be reduced, so higher solar light utilization efficiency is achieved [10,12]. By combining photothermal materials with “air-water interface solar heating concept”, portable solar driven water purification devices is available, intensive use of chemicals, fuels and capital can be avoid [11]. Until now, there are mainly three types of photothermal materials employed, which include carbon-based materials, plasmonic metals and semiconducting materials [12]. Black TiO2 can absorb part or entire part of solar energy, this is significant to solar-driven water evporation. By magnesium reduction, TiOx(x < 2) nanoparticles with tunable colors have been prepared for solar-driven water evporation, and a thermal efficiency of 50% under one sun irradiation was attained [13]. Huang et al. synthesized black TiO2 with unique nanocage structure for solar-driven water evaporation, the light to heat conversion efficiency reached to as high as 70.9% under one sun irradiation [14]. Nanostructured titanium oxide have become a potential material for solar-driven water evaporation. Superior water purification performance can be expected by developing the black titanium oxide materials with rationally designed phtocatalytic and photothermal properties.

With regarding to the titanium oxide materials, titanium oxide nanotubes with large specific surface area may be a desirable and alternative option for solar energy utilization, its special nanotubular structure is more appropriate for compounding with other materials. However, to the best of our knowledge, until now few reports consider titanium oxide nanotube as a potential and feasible material for photothermal applications. Research on the solar-driven water evaporation application of titanium oxide nanotube lay far behind other titanium oxide materials. Considering this, in order to exploit the potentialities of titanium oxide nanotube on utilizing solar light for pure water generation, in this study, we prepared TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam (TNA@Ti) and investigated its potential for photothermal and photocatalytic applications (Fig. 1 a and b). Ti foam was firstly anodized in fluoride containing ethylene glycol solution to produce TiO2 nanotube array on the surface. Black TiO2 nanotube array was then obtained through a mild hydrogenation method using NaBH4 as the H2 source. Finally, carbon dots were synthesized and anchored on black TiO2 nanotube array through electrochemical decomposition method. Compared to TNA@Ti, Ti foam with black TiO2 nanotube array (Black TNA@Ti) exhibited enhanced solar-driven water evaporation and photocatalytic abilities. By carbon dots decoration, the solar light utilization ability of Black TNA@Ti can be further enhanced. The research details for the stepwise enhancement of solar light utilization ability by hydrogenation and carbon dots decoration was discussed below.

Chemical reagents of analytical pure including ethylene glycol, ammonia fluoride, sodium borohydride, tert-butanol, ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid, ascorbic acid, methanol and acetone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd and used as received. Graphite rods were purchased from Tianjin Aida. Co., Ltd (purity
≥
99.9%, Ф = 6 mm), Ti foams were purchased from Kunshan Electronic Materials Co., Ltd (purity ≥ 99.5%, size: 0.2 cm × 2 cm × 1 cm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TNA@Ti and Black TNA@Ti
ce:section.ce:para: Using a two-electrodes setup, anodization method was used to prepare TiO2 nanotube array on the surface of Ti foam. Prior to anodization, Ti foams were ultrasonically degreased in acetone, methanol and purified water respectively. Ti foam and platinum sheet were used as anode and cathode electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte is composed of 57.7 g ethylene glycol, 1.2 g H2O and 0.12 g NH4F. With a stirring speed of 200 r min−1, the anodization experiment was carried out under 35 V for 100 min. After anodization, the anodized Ti foams were ultrasonically washed in methanol for 2 min to remove organic residues, and then dried under ambient air condition. Subsequently, the as prepared Ti foams were annealed at 500 °C for 2 h with heating rate of 2 °C min−1 in air condition. Finally, Ti foam with TiO2 nanotube array on the surface was obtained, and denoted as TNA@Ti.

Ti foam with black TiO2 nanotube array was prepared by mild hydrogenation method using NaBH4 as hydrogen source [15]. In the hydrogenation process, TNA@Ti and NaBH4 were separately placed in two quartz crucibles in a tube furnace. The crucible holding TNA@Ti was placed on top of the other holding NaBH4. Under a typical base pressure of ˂0.1 MPa, the tube furnace was heated to 500 °C and then kept for 4 h. Under 500 °C, NaBH4 will decompose, generate H2, and cause a mild hydrogenation of TiO2. In order to promote the solar light absorption ability, three different dosages of NaBH4 (100 mg, 200 mg and 300 mg) were selected, and the as-prepared samples were denoted as Black TNA-100@Ti, Black TNA-200@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti respectively.

Carbon dots were prepared by a facile electrochemical exfoliation method. In a beaker containing 100 mL purified water, two graphite rods were put parallel to each other with a distance of 2.5 cm, the applied voltage was kept at 15 V. After 120 h with continuous stirring of 200 r min−1, carbon dots solution was obtained. The resulted carbon dots solution was then centrifuged at 16000 rpm for 20 min to remove the precipitation, and then saved the supernatant as standby in ambient temperature.

Carbon dots were decorated on Black TNA@Ti by electrochemical deposition method under constant voltage of 5 V. The electrolyte was the above prepared standby carbon dots solution, the anode and cathode were Pt foil and Black TNA-300@Ti respectively, and the time was 15 min. After electrochemical deposition process, carbon dots decorated Black TNA@Ti was obtained, taken out and dried under ambient condition. To get suitable loading amount of carbon dots, electrochemical deposition experiment was repeated 5, 10, 20 and 30 times respectively, and the obtained samples were denoted as CDs-5/Black TNA-300@Ti, CDs-10/Black TNA-300@Ti, CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-30/Black TNA-300@Ti respectively.

The morphological details were obtained using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL). The crystal structure of samples were examined on an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, DX2700B, Haoyuan Instrument) at a scan rate of 0.08° s−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectra measurement was performed using a thermo scientific ESCALAB 250Xi XPS system using Al anode. Total porosity, channel size and area of Ti foam were evaluated with a mercury intrusion porosimeter (AutoPore IV 9500, Micromeritics). The optical characterization of samples was conducted using a UV–Vis spectrometer (Cary 5000, Agilent) with an integrating sphere attachment. Electron paramagnetic resonance spectra (EPR) were collected at 90 K on a Bruker EMX-8 spectrometer. The wettability of various samples was characterized using an optical contact angle meter system (OCA20, Dataphysics). The droplets used for the wettability measurement were 5 μL with a dosing rate of 1 μL s−1 at ambient environment. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Tensor27 FTIR spectrometer with a scan range of 2400−400 cm−1. Photocatalytic generated quantity of hydroxyl radicals was analysed by photoluminescence technique using terephthalic acid as probe molecule. The concentration of terephthalic acid was 4 × 10−4 mol L−1 in 4 × 10−3 mol L−1 NaOH solution. The photoluminescence spectra were measured with 315 nm excitation after 30 min simulated solar light illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-driven water evaporation and pollutant degradation tests
ce:section.ce:para: For solar driven water evaporation test, water was placed in a quartz container with square edge length of 1.4 cm and a depth of 4 cm. In order to minimize heat exchange with ambient air, the quartz container was wrapped with polystyrene foam. A xenon lamp (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT) was used to provide the simulated solar light irradiation, the light intensity was kept at 2 kW m−2 (two sun). The cylinder container was filled with 5 g of purified water, and located under the simulated solar light source with a distance of 16 cm (Fig. 1b). The as-prepared sample was put inside a teflon supporter with cotton thread pumping the water from bulk water below to sample surface. In order to real-time monitor the weight of evaporated water, the cylinder container was placed on an electronic analytical scale (EX223ZH, OHAUS). After solar irradiation for 30 min, the weight of evaporated water was recorded every 5 min for 1 h. The surface temperature of sample at the initial 30 min was measured using a thermocouple every minutes. The infrared images were also captured via a infrared camera (UTi80, UNT-T) after solar light irradiation of initial 30 min.

For photocatalytic pollutant degradation tests, the photocatalytic activities of various samples were evaluated by measuring the degradation rates of rodamine B solution (1.5 mg L−1). Intensity of simulated solar light was 121.5 mW cm−2. In order to achieve absorption equilibrium, samples were firstly immersed in a quartz cell containing 4 mL pollutant solution for 40 min under dark environment. Then the quartz cell was subjected to solar light irradiation to initiate the photocatalytic pollutant degradation reactions. The irradiation time was 1.5 h, and the absorbance of pollutant solution was measured every 30 min by a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV-2550, Shimada). The maximum absorption density centered at 554 nm was measured for calculating the degradation rate of rodamine B solution. The photocatalytic by-products of rhodamine B were detected by liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (LC/MS, Thermo, USA) equipped with an electrospray ionization (ESI) positive ion mode. A Beta Basic-18 column (150 × 2.1 mm, Thermo, USA) was used for chromatographically separation of samples. The flow rate was 1.0 mL/min, and the mobile phase was methanol-water (80:20, V/V).

Fig. 1c−f show the SEM images of Ti foam after anodization. It can be clearly observed that TiO2 nanotube array were formed on the surface of Ti foam after anodization. TiO2 nanotubes grown perpendicular to the rough/curved Ti surface, and thus the original porous structure of Ti foam was kept well after anodization, many internal channels of bare Ti foam still exist (Fig. 1c). With internal channels, water can be readily pumped from bulk region to hot zone during solar-driven water evaporation process, and the surface area for water evaporation and pollutant degradation can be enhanced. Notably, probably due to the different radius of curvature at the attachment point on nanotube wall, bundling of nanotube occurred during anodization process, many “crevasses” were formed in nanotube array (Fig. 1d). These “crevasses” are also somewhat benefit for the solar-driven water evaporation and photocatalysis, since they could facilitate the delivery of liquid inside nanotube array. In Fig. 1e and f, anodic nanotubes demonstrate an outer diameter of ∼60 nm and length of ∼3.5 μm. “Ripples” can be clearly observed on the outer wall of nanotube. According to P. Schmuki's reports, “fluoride-rich layer” could be formed and induce the formation of “ripples” during anodization process, the ripples work as joint between nanotubes, and robust nanotube array can be obtained [7,16]. The co-existence of nanotubes, “crevasses” and internal channels of Ti foams formed a unique hierarchical porous structure, which is significant for the design of functional materials. For this hierarchical porous structure, higher surface area can be obtained. As shown in Fig. S1, after anodization, due to the growth of nanotube arrays, internal channels of Ti foam became smaller, porosity increased from 25% to 31%, and the calculated channel area increased from 0.014 m2/g to 0.029 m2/g. The higher channel area and as-formed nanotubes can both enlarge the surface area of Ti foam. During the solar-driven water evaporation and pollutant degradation process, the enlarged surface area can efficiently enhance solar light trapping property and provide more active sites [17,18].

In order to optimize the solar light absorption property, different annealing methods were applied to the as-anodized Ti foam. Initially, for TNA@Ti obtained by annealing at 500 °C for 2 h in air condition, yellowish color appeared (Fig. 2 a). The UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of TNA@Ti showed an absorption edge at around 420 nm due to the intrinsic band gap absorption of TiO2 (Fig. 2c). However, when further subjected TNA@Ti to mild hydrogenation treatment using NaBH4 as hydrogen source, the color of TNA@Ti turned into black when the quantity of used NaBH4 increased to 300 mg (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1). In Fig. 2c, the sample of Black TNA-300@Ti exhibited much improved light absorption property in the region of 200–2400 nm, and an evident red shift of absorption edge is observed. This enhanced solar light absorption might be due to the crystalline and chemical defects induced by hydrogenation [19]. To confirm this, TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti were characterized using TEM, the obtained results are shown in Fig.s 2d−g, indicating different crystal features of TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti. For TNA@Ti, in Fig.s 2d and 2e, clear-resolved and well-defined lattice fringes are uniform throughout nanotube, even at the outer surface of nanotube. The distance between the adjacent lattice planes was ∼0.352 nm, which is typical character of anatase TiO2 [20]. While for Black TNA-300@Ti, structural deviation from standard crystalline anatase appeared at the outer surface. As shown in Fig. 2g, a lattice disordered layer can be readily seen at the edge of nanotube, and the crystalline plane was no longer recognizable. It is believed that the disordered layer hosts the possible hydrogen dopant, contributes to defect states, and induce remarkable band gap narrowing [21].

To further investigate the crystalline and chemical defects introduced by hydrogenation, the crystalline structure and chemical composition of TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti were next characterized. From the XRD patterns in Fig. 3 a, peaks belong to anatase is observable for both TNA@Ti and Black TNA-300@Ti. The difference is the peak intensity, anatase peak intensity of Black TNA-300@Ti was notably lower than TNA@Ti. This provides further evidence that hydrogenation created lattice disordered layer, and reduced the degree of crystallinity. It was also found that the lattice disordered layer is accompanied by the production of oxygen deficiency. As shown in the EPR spectra of Fig. 3b, Black TNA-300@Ti gives rise to a strong EPR signal with g = 2.002, which can be attributed to paramagnetic oxygen vacancies [22]. The oxygen vacancies can serve as electron traps, electrons bound to oxygen and titanium ions will migrate to oxygen vacancies, and the surface chemical bonding of TiO2 crystals will change [23]. In the high resolution XPS spectra of Fig. 3c, compared to TNA@Ti, Ti2p XPS profile is slightly shifted to lower energy for Black TNA-300@Ti, a similar shift can also be observed for O1s spectra of Fig. 3d. This is another clear evidence that oxygen vacancies were formed by hydrogenation treatment. The oxygen vacancies could be another reason for the extended solar light absorption ability of TiO2 nanotube array, because the oxygen vacancies give rise to the donor level below the conduction band edge [24,25].

Next step, carbon dots were prepared by electrochemical exfoliation method, and decorated on Black TNA@Ti through repetitive electrochemical deposition method. Fig. S3a shows the survey XPS spectra of samples with different repeat times (5, 10 and 20 times). Notably, at ∼284.8 eV, the C1s peak intensity increased monotonically with repeat times. Since carbon dots are the only carbon source during electrochemical deposition process, one can conclude that the quantity of decorated carbon dots increased with repeat times. TEM images provide direct evidence on the monotonically increased loading amount of carbon dots. In Figs. S3b and S3c, for both CDs-5/Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-10/Black TNA-300@Ti, carbon dots on nanotube array are rare and sporadic. While for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, significant amounts of carbon dots can be readily observed with uniform dispersion (Fig. 4 a). Nevertheless, the channels of nanotube array was kept well for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, suggesting 20 times' electrochemical deposition has neglectable effect on nanotube structure. Excessive deposition times may deteriorate the uniformity of carbon dots' distribution. As shown in Fig. S4, agglomeration of carbon dots appeared for 30 times' electrochemical deposition. The agglomerates of carbon dots may block the interaction between solar light and black TiO2, and cause adverse effect on the solar light utilization ability of Black TiO2 [26]. Fig. 4b shows the high resolution TEM image of CDs-20/Black TNA@Ti, wherein lattice spacing of 0.238 nm and 0.352 nm can be clearly observed, they correspond to the (1120) plane of graphene and (101) plane of anatase TiO2 [27,28]. In Fig.s 4c and 4d, the energy dispersive spectroscopy maps in STEM mode show that CDs-20/Black TNA@Ti contains Ti, O and C elements, and preferential segregation of carbon doesn't exist. Based on above results, it can be concluded that effective decoration of carbon dots on Black TNA-300@Ti can be achieved by 20 times' electrochemical deposition, a considerable amounts of carbon dots can be decorated on black TiO2 without agglomeration.

Fig. 5 a demonstrates the UV–vis–NIR spectrum of Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, it is clear that CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti showed enhanced absorption from UV to NIR compared to Black TNA-300@Ti. This suggests that CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti would be excellent photo-absorbing agents for solar light utilization. The possible reason could be the interaction between TiO2 and carbon dots. For TiO2/carbon dots compounds, the carboxylic acid functional groups on graphene quantum dots could interact with the surface hydroxyl groups of TiO2, and Ti–O–C bonds can be formed between TiO2 and carbon dots [29]. As shown in Fig. S5, the high resolution C1s spectrum of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti was deconvoluted, and the peak located at 530.65 eV can be attributed to Ti–O–C bonds [30]. The Ti–O–C bonds can introduce additional energy states in between the conduction and valence bands of TiO2 and carbon dots [31]. Besides the solar light absorption ability, the wettability of black TiO2 nanotube array was also compared before and after carbon dots decoration. In order to prevent water infiltration on Ti foam, black TiO2 nanotube array was prepared on Ti foil for comparation, morphologies and detailed experimental details for black TiO2 nanotube array can be found in supporting information (Fig. S9). In Fig. 5b, without carbon dots decoration, the surface of black TiO2 nanotube array was hydrophilic with contact angle of ∼55.6°. The contact angle was significantly reduced when carbon dots were decorated on black TiO2 nanotube array, it turns into ∼18.8° in Fig. 5c. Through electrochemical oxidation of graphite rods, many hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups were formed for carbon dots. As shown in Fig. S6b of FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots, peaks located at 3435 cm−1 and 1718 cm−1, which correspond to hydroxyl OH and carboxyl C=O can be observed clearly [32,33]. When carbon dots are decorated on Black TiO2 nanotube array, these hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups can improve the surface hydrophility. Moreover, it can be expected that surface roughness could be increased for the decoration of nanosized carbon dots [34]. Based on the equation proposed by Wenzel model [35],
cosθ′ = rcosθ

where θ′ and θ are the apparent water contact angle on a rough and flat TiO2 surface respectively. The factor r is the roughness ratio, which is defined as the ratio of true area of solid surface to apparent area. The increased surface roughness by carbon dots decoration could also decrease the water contact angle. Therefore, carbon dots is also capable of improving the hydrophility of black TiO2 nanotube array. For this reason, wettability of Black TNA@Ti was improved by carbon dots decoration, water droplet infiltrates faster on CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti compared to Black TNA@Ti (videos in the supporting information). During solar-driven water evaporation process, the enhanced wettability could facilitate the pumping of water from bulk water blow to surface hot zone, one can expect that the water evaporation can be accelerated.

Over the investigation of light harvesting and wetting abilities of various samples, artificial solar-driven water evaporation experiments were next conducted upon 2 kW m−2 simulated solar light irradiation, and the temperature increment of sample surface was initially monitored. In Fig. 6 a, after irradiation for 30 min, elevation of surface temperature is 25.6 °C for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, which is much higher than that of bulk water (4.9 °C), TNA@Ti (11.2 °C) and Black TNA-300@Ti (17.5 °C) (Table S1). The same results were demonstrated in IR thermal images of Fig.s 6b−6e. For CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, top surface temperature as high as ∼48.2 °C was achieved after solar irradiation of 30 min While for bulk water, TNA@Ti and Black TNA@Ti, top surface temperature were just 38.4 °C, 31.5 °C and 25.3 °C respectively. This indicates excellent photothermal conversion ability of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti. One reason should be the broad and strong solar light absorption of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti. A larger proportion of solar energy can be absorbed for generating thermal energy. Another reason could be the narrowed bandgap of TiO2 for the hydrogenation treatment and carbon dots decoration, more above-bandgap electron-hole pairs will be generated, relax to band gap edges and convert solar energy into heat [36]. Moreover, as shown in Fig.s 4a and 4b, CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti can form CDs/Black TNA composite on the surface. compared to pure Black TNA, this CDs/Black TNA composite could result in remarkable increase in thermal conductivity, facilitate large-scale interfacial heating, and give rise to top surface temperature [37].

For evaluating solar-driven water evaporation efficiency, the weight loss of water was surveyed, and the results are shown in Fig.s 6f, S7 and S8. Firstly, the evaporated water at 1 h for every Ti foam-based samples is significantly higher than bulk water only, indicating the improved solar-driven water evaporation performance by using Ti foam-based samples. When using Ti foam-based samples for solar-driven water evaporation, there are two unique features to be noted: (i) the hierarchical porous structure can enhance solar light trapping ability and water evaporation area, (ii) the indirect contact between Ti foam and bulk water can reduce heat transfer from surface evaporation region to underneath non-evaporation region. Due to above two features, weight loss of water was significantly improved when using Ti foam-based samples. Secondly, as anticipated, CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti demonstrated the highest mass change of water. In Fig. 6g and Table S1, the calculated water evaporation rate was 1.762 kg m−2 h−1 for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, which is much higher than other Ti foam-based samples. When subjected to solar light irradiation, the highest top surface temperature was achieved for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, and contribute to the highest water evaporation rate. In addition, as discussed above, wettability of black TiO2 nanotube array was improved by carbon dots decoration (Fig.s 5b and 5c). The improved wettability can accelerate water flow from bulk water to hot zone of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, allow continuous vapor generation on the surface, and improve water evaporation rate. To further quantitatively investigate the photothermal properties of various samples, the corresponding solar thermal conversion efficiency for solar-driven water evporation test was calculated using the following formula [38]:
η = Qe/Qs
where Q
s is the incident power density of light source (2 kW m−2), Q
e is the power density of water evaporation. Q
e can be estimated using equation below [39]:
Qe = dm × He/dt = v × He

Here, m is the mass of evaporated water, t is the time, v is the evaporation rate of water and H
e is the heat of evaporation of water (∼2260 kJ kg−1). The calculated results are shown in Fig. 6g and Table S1. It can be found that the solar thermal conversion efficiency for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti reached to 55.3% under 2 kW m−2 simulated solar light irradiation. This is also higher than every other Ti foam-based samples. Consequently, through successive anodization, hydrogenation and carbon dots decoration, the solar-driven water evporation was stepwise accelerated. Among all the prepared samples, the sample of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti manifested the highest water evporation rate and solar thermal conversion efficiency (1.762 kg m−2 h−1 and 55.3%). These values are also higher than our previous reports on titanium oxide nanotube. Previously, reduced TiO2 nanotube array was prepared on Ti gauze for solar driven water evaporation, water evaporation rate and solar thermal conversion efficiency of 1.41 kg m−2 h−1 and 44.3% was achieved [40]. Herein, the new adopted porous Ti foam and carbon dots could benefit more for the solar light absorption ability and wettability of titanium oxide nanotube, the water evaporation rate and solar thermal conversion efficiency were thus further improved for titanium oxide nanotube in this work.

Besides the photothermal property, photocatalytic properties of photofading, TNA@Ti, Black TNA-300@Ti and CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti were also investigated and compared using rhodamine B as degradation object. Since the decomposition kinetics of rhodamine B follows the first order law of ln(C/C0) = kt, where k is the pesudo-first-order rate constant and t is the time [41], the time-dependent rhodamine B concentration was initially plotted on a logarithmic scale, and the corresponding rate constants were then determined by the slope of decay fits (Fig.s 7a and 7b). The results in Fig. 7 a indicate that CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti possesses the highest photocatalytic activity. The calculated degradation rate of rhodamine B for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti is 0.8694 h−1, which is significantly higher than other samples (0.1334 h−1 for photofading, 0.3268 h−1 for TNA@Ti, 0.5236 h−1 for Black TNA-300@Ti). The enhanced photocatalytic activity should be attributed to both the hydrogenation treatment and carbon dots decoration. As discussed above, through hydrogenation and carbon dots decoration, solar light absorption ability was improved for the introduction of inter-band states. Due to the conductive band position of carbon dots is more negative than that of black TiO2, holes generated in the valence band of black TiO2 will transfer to carbon dots, while electrons will accumulate on black TiO2. So composite of black TiO2 nanotube and carbon dots can promote the interface charge transfer and separation during photocatalytic process [42]. For above two reasons, more photo-generated reactive oxygen species (e.g., hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals) will be generated under solar light irradiation according to the equation below [43]:
CDs(h+) + OH− → CDs + OH•
TiO2(e−) + O2 → TiO2 + O2 −
O2 − + H2O → H2O2 + OH•
H2O2 + e− → OH• + OH−

This is confirmed in Fig.s 7b and 7c. Fig. 7b shows the effects of ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA, hole scavenger), tert-butanol (hydroxyl radicals quencher) and ascorbic acid (superoxide quencher) on the degradation rate of rhodamine B in the presence of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti. For ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid and ascorbic acid, only moderate suppression on the degradation efficiency of rhodamine B was achieved (k = 0.7199 h−1 and 0.6436 h−1). While for tert-butanol, degradation rate of rhodamine B was maximally reduced to only 0.3622 h−1, implying hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are the primary active species for photocatalytic system of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti. This is consistent with the •OH predominant photocatalytic system of TiO2 nanotube [44]. We then detected and compared the amounts of •OH produced in the presence of various samples using terephthalic acid solution. Chemical reaction between terephthalic acid and •OH will produce 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid with significant fluorescence, so the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the amount of produced •OH [45]. In Fig. 7c, the highest PL intensity is observed for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, indicating more •OH were generated by CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti compared to other samples. •OH is strong oxidizing agent for rhodamine B degradation, so CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti achieved much improved photocatalytic performance for rhodamine B degradation.

Investigation of the by-products in photocatalytic process was also conducted based on temporal UV–visible spectral changes and LC-MS measurements of rhodamine B solution. As shown in Fig. S10, decrease in characteristic absorption band of rhodamine B at 554 nm can be clearly observed, indicating gradual degradation of rhodamine B by CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti under solar light irradiation. Notably, hypsochromic shifts of the absorption maximum is obvious. These shifts result from the formation of a series of N-deethylated intermediates of rhodamine B [46]. During photocatalytic process, the sequential removal of the N-ethyl groups from rhodamine B molecule will produce five compounds with less N-ethyl (N,N-diethyl-N′-ethylrhodamine (DER), N-ethyl-N′-ethylrhodamine (EER), N,N-diethylrhodamine (DR), N-ethylrhodamine (ER) and rhodamine (R)) [47]. Since the N-ethyl group has auxochromic property, the characteristic absorption of intermediates with less N-ethyl occurred at shorter wavelength (Fig. S10). The formation of N-deethylated intermediates was also confirmed by LC-MS measurement. In mass spectra of Fig. S11, mass peaks at m/z 443, 415, 387, 359, 331 can be identified and attributed to rhodamine B and its N-deethylated intermediates [48]. In addition to the N-deethylation process, the photogenerated active species such as •OH or hole can also attack the ring of rhodamine B and/or N-deethylated intermediates, cleavage of chromophore will occur and produce small molecular intermediates. As shown in Fig. S11c, mass peaks with short retention time can be clearly observed, they could belong to the small molecular intermediates such as oxalic acid (m/z = 90), 3-Nitrobenzoic acid (m/z = 167) et al. [49]. Based on above discussions, the sample of CDs-20/Black TNA-200@Ti can degrade rhodamine B efficiently under solar light irradiation, the degradation of rhodamine B involved both the N-deethylation process and cleavage of chromophore, and the small mass molecules could be further transformed to water and carbon dioxide through mineralization in the end.

With regarding to the stability of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti, the cycling solar driven water evaporation and photocatalytic experiments were further conducted. In Fig. 8 a, it can be clearly observed that the water evaporation rate and Rhodamine B degradation rate remains stable after 20 times cycling experiment. This indicates that the photothermal and photocatalytic properties of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti are stable for reuse. The chemical composition and morphology were also measured for CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti after 20 times cycling experiment. In XPS spectra of Fig. 8b, wide-scanning XPS profiles are similar before and after 20 times cycling experiment. In TEM image of Fig. 8c, nanoporous structure with uniform distribution of carbon dots can be clearly observed. Based on above results, it can be concluded that compounds in the sample of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti can hardly loss during solar driven water treatment process, morphological change is negligible. The stability of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti is benefit for long term practical applications in area of solar driven water treatment.

In summary, we for the first time prepared carbon dots decorated black TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam for both photothermal and photocatalytic applications. Initially, the sample of black TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam was obtained through anodization followed by hydrogenation using 300 mg NaBH4 as hydrogen source. Black TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam demonstrated hierarchical porous structure, and the hydrogenation treatment created crystalline disordered layer and oxygen vacncies inside TiO2. These are benefit for the improved solar light absorption. Next step, carbon dots were decorated on nanotube with high uniformity by electrochemical deposition method. It was found that the solar light absorption ability and wettability of black TiO2 nanotube array@Ti foam can be further enhanced by carbon dots decoration. Owning to the gradually increased surface area, light absorption ability and wettability, the solar light utilization ability of TiO2 nanotube-based device was stepwise improved. Under 2 kW m−2 solar light irradiation, among all the as-prepared samples, the highest photothermal and photocatalytic properties were both achieved for sample of CDs-20/Black TNA-300@Ti. This work may provide guideline and effective strategy to prepare TiO2 nanotube-based devices for promising applications in solar-driven water purification and environmental remediation.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51602292), Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (Grant No. 201701D221087) and the starting fund for scientific research of North University of China (Grant No.130082).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.I. Ishibashi, A. Fujishima, T. Watanabe, K. Hashimoto, Detection of active oxidative species in TiO2 photocatalysis using the fluorescence technique, Electrochem. Commun. 2 (2000) 207−210.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B by Bi2WO6 with electron accepting agent under microwave irradiation: mechanism and pathway

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. He, C. Sun, S. Yang, Y. Ding, H. He, Z. Wang, Photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B by Bi2WO6 with electron accepting agent under microwave irradiation: mechanism and pathway, J. Hazard. Mater. 162 (2009) 1477−1486.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on UV-LED/TiO2 process for degradation of rhodamine B dye

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. S. Natarajan, M. Thomas, K. Natarajan, H. C. Bajaj, R. J. Tayade, Study on UV-LED/TiO2 process for degradation of rhodamine B dye, Chem. Eng. J. 169 (2011) 126−134.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible light-driven photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B over NaBiO3: pathways and mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.Yu, S.Yang, H.He, C.Sun, C. Gu, Y. Ju, Visible light-driven photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B over NaBiO3: pathways and mechanism, J. Phys. Chem. A 113 (2009) 10024−10032.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.Sun, D. Li, Y.Chen, W. Chen, W. Li, Y. He, X. Fu, Synthesis and photocatalytic activity of cacium antimony oxide hydroxide for the degradation of dyes in water, J. Phys. Chem. C 113 (2009) 13825−13831.
